name: Broken Access Control
description: Broken Access Control is the most critical web application security risk, moving up from fifth position in OWASP 2017. It occurs when users can act outside their intended permissions, including accessing other users' data, modifying access rights, or performing unauthorized functions. Common vulnerabilities include IDOR (Insecure Direct Object References) where attackers modify resource identifiers to access other users' records, missing function-level access control allowing regular users to access admin endpoints, path traversal exposing files outside the web root, CORS misconfiguration enabling unauthorized cross-origin access, and metadata manipulation (JWT tokens, cookies, hidden fields) to elevate privileges. The shift to API-first architectures has expanded the attack surface significantly, as APIs often expose more granular access control decisions than traditional web applications.

name: Cryptographic Failures
description: Previously known as Sensitive Data Exposure, this category focuses on failures related to cryptography (or lack thereof) that lead to sensitive data exposure. This includes transmitting data in cleartext (HTTP, SMTP without TLS, FTP), using deprecated cryptographic algorithms (MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4), employing weak or default cryptographic keys, failing to enforce encryption through missing HTTP security headers (HSTS), using improper certificate validation, and storing passwords with fast hashing algorithms instead of adaptive functions like bcrypt or Argon2. Data in transit and at rest must be classified by sensitivity level with appropriate cryptographic protections applied. Regulatory requirements (GDPR, PCI-DSS, HIPAA) mandate specific encryption standards for different data categories, and failure to comply results in both security breaches and regulatory penalties.

name: Injection
description: Injection flaws occur when hostile data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query, tricking the interpreter into executing unintended commands or accessing data without proper authorization. This category now includes Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in addition to traditional injection types. Major injection variants include SQL injection (manipulating database queries), OS command injection (executing system commands), LDAP injection (altering directory queries), XPath/XML injection, NoSQL injection (MongoDB operator injection), ORM injection (HQL, JPQL), expression language injection (Spring EL, OGNL), and server-side template injection (Jinja2, Twig, Freemarker). The primary defense is using parameterized interfaces that separate code from data, supplemented by input validation, output encoding, and least-privilege database accounts. Automated testing tools can detect many injection variants but manual testing remains essential for complex business logic.

name: Insecure Design
description: A new category in 2021, Insecure Design focuses on risks related to design and architectural flaws rather than implementation bugs. It calls for greater use of threat modeling, secure design patterns, and reference architectures. Insecure design cannot be fixed by a perfect implementation because the fundamental security controls were never created. Examples include a password recovery system that uses knowledge-based questions (inherently weak regardless of implementation quality), a booking system without rate limiting that enables denial-of-inventory attacks, an API that returns more data than the client needs (over-fetching enabling data harvesting), and systems lacking multi-tenancy isolation at the design level. Addressing insecure design requires establishing a secure development lifecycle with threat modeling at the design phase, security requirements derived from business risk analysis, and secure design patterns for common security scenarios.

name: Security Misconfiguration
description: Security Misconfiguration encompasses a wide range of configuration-level security failures across the entire application stack. This includes default credentials on administrative interfaces, unnecessary features enabled (ports, services, pages, accounts, privileges), verbose error messages exposing stack traces and internal details, missing security headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS), disabled security features in frameworks, misconfigured cloud storage permissions (public S3 buckets), XML processor settings allowing XXE, and unpatched or outdated server software. With the shift to cloud-native architectures, misconfiguration risk has expanded to include container configurations, Kubernetes security contexts, serverless function permissions, and infrastructure-as-code templates. Automated security scanning, hardened baseline configurations, and regular configuration audits are essential preventive measures.

name: Vulnerable and Outdated Components
description: This risk addresses the use of software components (libraries, frameworks, OS components) that have known security vulnerabilities or are no longer maintained. Modern applications typically include dozens to hundreds of dependencies, each potentially introducing vulnerabilities through their own dependency trees (transitive dependencies). High-profile incidents include Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) affecting millions of Java applications, the Equifax breach via Apache Struts (CVE-2017-5638), and Spring4Shell (CVE-2022-22965). Organizations must maintain a complete inventory of all component versions (Software Bill of Materials), implement automated vulnerability scanning in CI/CD pipelines using tools like OWASP Dependency-Check, Snyk, or Trivy, subscribe to security advisories, and establish policies for timely patching. Components that are no longer maintained should be replaced as they will accumulate unpatched vulnerabilities.

name: Identification and Authentication Failures
description: Previously called Broken Authentication, this category covers weaknesses in the identification and authentication process that allow attackers to compromise passwords, keys, or session tokens, or to impersonate other users. Common failures include permitting credential stuffing and brute-force attacks due to missing rate limiting, allowing weak or well-known passwords, using plaintext or weakly hashed password storage, missing or ineffective multi-factor authentication, exposing session identifiers in URLs, session fixation attacks, and improper session invalidation on logout or timeout. API-based authentication adds risks around insecure token generation, missing token expiration, JWT algorithm confusion attacks, and API key exposure. Prevention requires robust authentication mechanisms, MFA for sensitive operations, secure session management, and monitoring for anomalous authentication patterns.

name: Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: A new category in 2021, this addresses failures to protect against integrity violations in software updates, critical data, and CI/CD pipelines. The SolarWinds supply chain attack (2020) exemplified this risk, where backdoored updates were signed and distributed through legitimate channels. Key concerns include software updates applied without integrity verification, applications relying on plugins or libraries from untrusted sources (CDNs, public registries without SRI), CI/CD pipelines without proper access controls allowing injection of malicious code, and insecure deserialization where untrusted serialized data is processed. Prevention requires verifying digital signatures on software updates, using trusted repositories with integrity verification (lock files, SRI hashes), implementing code signing, applying least privilege to CI/CD systems, and validating all serialized data from untrusted sources.

name: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
description: Insufficient logging, monitoring, and incident response capabilities allow attackers to operate undetected, maintain persistence, pivot to other systems, and extract or destroy data. The average time to detect a breach exceeds 200 days in many organizations. Key failures include not logging security-relevant events (login attempts, access control failures, input validation errors), log entries lacking sufficient detail (no timestamps, source IPs, user identifiers), logs stored only locally and not aggregated centrally, alerts not being generated for suspicious activity patterns, incident response plans not existing or not being tested, and penetration tests not triggering alerts. Effective security monitoring requires centralized log aggregation, real-time alerting on suspicious patterns, integration with SIEM/SOAR platforms, regular testing of detection capabilities through red team exercises, and defined incident response procedures.

name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: SSRF vulnerabilities occur when a web application fetches a remote resource based on a user-supplied URL without properly validating the destination. New in the 2021 Top 10, SSRF has become significantly more impactful with cloud adoption because cloud metadata services (accessible at 169.254.169.254) often contain IAM credentials, instance configuration, and network information. Attackers exploit SSRF to access internal services behind firewalls, read cloud credentials from metadata endpoints (enabling full cloud account takeover), scan internal networks, access internal admin panels, and exploit internal services through protocol smuggling. Bypass techniques include IP address encoding variations (decimal, octal, hex), DNS rebinding, URL parser inconsistencies, and redirect chains. Defense requires network-level egress filtering, URL validation against allowlists, disabling unnecessary URL schemes, and limiting cloud metadata service access through IMDSv2.

name: Broken Object Level Authorization
description: The most prevalent API vulnerability, Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) occurs when APIs expose object identifiers in requests without verifying that the authenticated user has permission to access the specific object. API architectures inherently expose resource identifiers in endpoints (GET /api/users/{id}/orders), making them natural targets for enumeration. Attackers modify object IDs in API requests to access other users' data, financial records, medical information, or any resources referenced by predictable identifiers. Unlike web applications where the UI may limit visible data, APIs typically return complete data objects, amplifying the impact. Testing requires systematic enumeration of object IDs across different user contexts. Prevention requires implementing authorization checks at the object level on every endpoint, using non-sequential or opaque identifiers, and centralizing authorization logic to ensure consistent enforcement.

name: Broken Authentication
description: API authentication is inherently more complex than traditional web authentication because APIs must support multiple authentication mechanisms (API keys, OAuth tokens, JWTs, mutual TLS) across diverse client types (mobile apps, SPAs, server-to-server, IoT devices). Common authentication failures include weak API key generation, missing token expiration, JWT algorithm confusion attacks (accepting 'none' or switching from RS256 to HS256), bearer tokens transmitted without TLS, missing brute-force protection on authentication endpoints, and credential stuffing against login APIs. Machine-to-machine authentication often relies on long-lived API keys or client credentials that lack rotation policies and are stored insecurely. Prevention requires using standard authentication protocols (OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect), implementing short-lived tokens with rotation, applying rate limiting to all authentication endpoints, and establishing credential rotation policies.

name: Broken Object Property Level Authorization
description: This merged category combines the former Mass Assignment and Excessive Data Exposure into a unified property-level authorization risk. Excessive Data Exposure occurs when API responses include more properties than the client needs, relying on client-side filtering rather than server-side enforcement. Mass Assignment occurs when APIs automatically bind client-provided data to internal object properties without restricting which properties can be set. Attackers exploit excessive exposure by inspecting API responses to discover sensitive fields not shown in the UI. They exploit mass assignment by adding properties like isAdmin, role, price, or accountBalance to requests. Prevention requires defining explicit response schemas that include only necessary properties (avoiding generic object serialization), implementing property-level allowlists for data binding, and performing thorough API response review to identify over-exposed data.

name: Unrestricted Resource Consumption
description: APIs are vulnerable to resource exhaustion attacks when they do not impose limits on client resource consumption. This includes missing rate limiting (allowing unlimited requests per time period), unbounded query complexity (GraphQL queries requesting deeply nested data), unlimited response sizes (returning millions of records), unrestricted file upload sizes, excessive operations in a single request (batch operations), and no limits on third-party API spending triggered by user actions. Without proper resource controls, a single attacker can exhaust API server capacity, generate excessive cloud billing costs, or degrade service for all users. Prevention requires implementing rate limiting per client/API key, setting maximum request sizes and query complexity limits, paginating responses with reasonable page sizes, and monitoring resource consumption patterns for anomalies.

name: Broken Function Level Authorization
description: APIs tend to expose more endpoints than traditional web applications, making function-level access control more complex and error-prone. This vulnerability occurs when regular users can access administrative API functions, or lower-privilege roles can invoke higher-privilege operations. Common patterns include administrative endpoints accessible to unauthenticated users (/api/admin/users), HTTP method confusion (PUT/DELETE available when only GET was intended), missing role checks on sensitive operations, and reliance on client-side UI restrictions rather than server-side enforcement. API documentation tools (Swagger/OpenAPI) may inadvertently expose administrative endpoints to developers and testers who then fail to restrict them. Prevention requires implementing a consistent authorization module that is invoked on every function, following the principle of least privilege for all API roles, and regularly auditing the API surface for unauthorized endpoint exposure.

name: Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows
description: A new category in API Security 2023, this addresses the risk of APIs being exploited through automated access to business flows in ways that harm the business. Unlike technical vulnerabilities, these attacks abuse legitimate API functionality at scale. Examples include automated ticket/product purchasing (scalping bots buying all concert tickets), automated account creation for spam campaigns, mass content scraping for competitive intelligence, automated referral/reward program abuse, and API-driven denial-of-inventory attacks (adding all items to cart without purchasing). Traditional security controls (WAFs, rate limiting) are insufficient because individual requests appear legitimate. Defense requires understanding which business flows are sensitive, implementing device fingerprinting and behavioral analysis, CAPTCHA/proof-of-work challenges, and business logic rate limiting that considers the business context rather than just request rates.

name: Server Side Request Forgery
description: SSRF in the API context is particularly dangerous because APIs frequently fetch external resources as part of their core functionality (webhook delivery, URL preview generation, file import from URLs, integration with third-party services). Attackers exploit these features to make the server send requests to internal infrastructure, cloud metadata endpoints, and other targets not directly accessible. API-specific SSRF vectors include webhook URLs pointing to internal services, file import features accepting arbitrary URLs, URL preview/unfurling features, and integration configuration endpoints. The API context makes SSRF more impactful because API servers often have broader network access to internal services and cloud infrastructure than web application servers. Prevention requires validating and sanitizing all client-supplied URLs, implementing allowlists for permitted destination domains, disabling URL redirects in server-side HTTP clients, and using network-level egress controls.

name: Security Misconfiguration
description: API security misconfiguration encompasses a broad range of configuration failures specific to API infrastructure. This includes missing TLS on API endpoints, permissive CORS policies (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *) on sensitive APIs, verbose error messages exposing API internals and stack traces, missing or permissive Content-Type validation, unnecessary HTTP methods enabled (TRACE, OPTIONS returning excessive information), missing security headers, misconfigured API gateways that bypass authentication, debug endpoints left active in production, and excessive API versioning exposing deprecated endpoints with known vulnerabilities. The rapid proliferation of microservices and API gateways creates a complex configuration surface where a single misconfiguration can expose multiple backend services. Automated configuration scanning, infrastructure-as-code with security policies, and API gateway hardening templates help maintain secure configurations.

name: Improper Inventory Management
description: APIs proliferate rapidly in modern architectures, and organizations frequently lose track of which APIs exist, which versions are active, what data they expose, and who has access. This creates a shadow API problem where undocumented, deprecated, or development APIs remain accessible in production without proper security controls. Common issues include deprecated API versions still accessible (v1 with known vulnerabilities alongside secure v2), development or staging API endpoints accessible externally, undocumented internal APIs exposed through API gateways, APIs with unclear data classification allowing sensitive data to flow through unprotected endpoints, and missing API lifecycle management. Prevention requires maintaining an up-to-date API inventory, implementing API discovery tools, establishing API versioning and deprecation policies, applying consistent security policies across all API versions, and regular auditing of exposed API endpoints versus the documented inventory.

name: Unsafe Consumption of APIs
description: This category addresses the risk of blindly trusting data received from third-party APIs without proper validation, treating external API responses as trusted input. Developers often apply less security rigor to data received from integrated third-party services compared to direct user input, creating injection and data manipulation opportunities. An attacker who compromises a third-party API or performs a man-in-the-middle attack can inject malicious data that flows through the consuming API without validation. Specific risks include trusting third-party API data in SQL queries (enabling SQL injection through the integration), following redirects from third-party responses (SSRF through redirect chains), and processing unvalidated data in security-sensitive operations. Prevention requires treating all external API responses as untrusted user input, validating and sanitizing data from integrations, implementing timeout and resource limits for third-party calls, and maintaining awareness of the security posture of integrated services.

name: Improper Credential Usage
description: This risk addresses how mobile applications handle credentials including API keys, tokens, passwords, and cryptographic secrets. Mobile apps frequently hardcode credentials in source code or compiled binaries where they can be extracted through reverse engineering (using tools like jadx, Hopper, or IDA Pro). Common failures include embedding API keys directly in application code, storing authentication tokens in insecure locations (SharedPreferences without encryption, NSUserDefaults), transmitting credentials in plaintext, failing to implement proper token refresh mechanisms, and using shared credentials across all app installations. The mobile context makes this particularly dangerous because every app installation is a potential extraction point for attackers. Prevention requires using secure credential storage mechanisms (Android Keystore, iOS Keychain), implementing OAuth 2.0 with PKCE for mobile authentication, avoiding credential embedding in client code, and using per-device unique credentials.

name: Inadequate Supply Chain Security
description: Mobile applications rely heavily on third-party SDKs, libraries, and development tools that introduce supply chain risks specific to the mobile ecosystem. Risks include malicious or compromised SDKs (advertising SDKs exfiltrating data, analytics SDKs with tracking beyond stated purposes), vulnerable open-source libraries in the dependency tree, compromised build tools or CI/CD pipelines injecting malicious code, and use of unmaintained libraries with known vulnerabilities. The mobile ecosystem amplifies supply chain risk because SDKs often request broad permissions, custom app stores may distribute trojanized versions, and app binary analysis by users is difficult. Prevention requires vetting all third-party SDKs for security and privacy compliance, maintaining a software bill of materials for mobile apps, implementing dependency scanning in mobile CI/CD pipelines, monitoring SDK behavior in production using network traffic analysis, and using code signing to ensure build integrity.

name: Insecure Authentication/Authorization
description: Mobile authentication and authorization present unique challenges due to offline usage patterns, biometric authentication, device-based trust models, and the split between client-side and server-side security enforcement. Common failures include relying solely on client-side authentication checks that can be bypassed through binary modification, insecure biometric implementation (using local-only biometric checks without cryptographic binding to server authentication), missing authorization checks on backend APIs that trust the mobile client to enforce access control, session management without proper token expiration, and failure to re-authenticate for sensitive operations. Mobile-specific attack vectors include runtime manipulation using Frida or Objection to bypass authentication logic, proxy interception to replay authentication tokens, and device rooting/jailbreaking to access stored credentials. Prevention requires server-side authentication enforcement, cryptographically bound biometric authentication, and treating the mobile client as an untrusted entity.

name: Insufficient Input/Output Validation
description: Mobile applications process input from multiple sources (user input, deep links, IPC intents, clipboard, NFC, QR codes, Bluetooth) and must validate all inputs regardless of source. Insufficient validation leads to SQL injection in local SQLite databases, path traversal accessing files outside the app sandbox, JavaScript injection through WebView interfaces, intent injection through exported Android components, URL scheme hijacking through custom protocol handlers, and buffer overflows in native code (C/C++ via JNI or Objective-C). Mobile-specific concerns include deep link parameter injection, clipboard data manipulation, and inter-process communication (Content Providers, Broadcast Receivers, Services) accepting malicious input from other apps. Prevention requires validating all input from every source, using parameterized queries for local database access, sanitizing data displayed in WebViews, restricting exported components with appropriate permission attributes, and implementing input validation for custom URL schemes.

name: Insecure Communication
description: Mobile devices frequently connect through untrusted networks (public WiFi, cellular networks susceptible to IMSI catchers) making secure communication critical. Common failures include transmitting sensitive data over HTTP, accepting invalid or self-signed certificates (disabling certificate validation for development and not re-enabling for production), missing certificate pinning allowing man-in-the-middle attacks with rogue CA certificates, using older TLS versions (TLS 1.0/1.1) with known vulnerabilities, and exposing data through cleartext traffic in background sync, analytics, or advertising SDKs. Mobile-specific complications include the need to balance security with performance on constrained devices and networks, handling certificate rotation with pinning, and managing multiple network interfaces. Prevention requires enforcing TLS for all communications, implementing certificate pinning with backup keys and rotation strategy, configuring Android Network Security Config and iOS ATS appropriately, and auditing all third-party SDK network traffic.

name: Inadequate Privacy Controls
description: Mobile applications have access to extensive personal data including location tracking, contacts, photos, camera, microphone, health data, and usage patterns. Inadequate privacy controls occur when apps collect more data than needed (violating data minimization principles), share personal data with third-party SDKs without user consent, fail to provide mechanisms for data deletion or export, store personal data longer than necessary, do not properly anonymize or aggregate analytics data, and expose personal information through insecure logging or crash reports. Regulatory frameworks (GDPR, CCPA, LGPD) impose specific requirements for mobile data handling including explicit consent, purpose limitation, data portability, and the right to erasure. Prevention requires implementing privacy by design, minimizing data collection, providing granular privacy controls to users, conducting privacy impact assessments, and regularly auditing data flows to and from the mobile application.

name: Insufficient Binary Protections
description: Mobile application binaries can be downloaded, reverse engineered, modified, and redistributed, creating risks for intellectual property theft, credential extraction, license bypass, and malicious repackaging. Android APKs and iOS IPAs can be decompiled using tools like jadx, apktool, Hopper, and Ghidra to recover source code, embedded secrets, and business logic. Attackers use dynamic instrumentation (Frida, Xposed, Cycript) to modify app behavior at runtime, bypassing authentication, premium content checks, and security controls. Code obfuscation (ProGuard/R8 for Android, bitcode for iOS) provides basic protection but can be defeated with sufficient effort. Root/jailbreak detection, integrity checking, anti-tampering mechanisms, and debugger detection raise the bar but are not foolproof. Critical business logic and secrets should remain server-side rather than relying on client-side binary protections.

name: Security Misconfiguration
description: Mobile application misconfiguration spans the mobile client, backend services, and platform-specific security features. Common misconfigurations include exported Android components (Activities, Content Providers, Broadcast Receivers) allowing unauthorized access, disabled iOS App Transport Security (ATS) permitting cleartext traffic, backup inclusion of sensitive data (android:allowBackup=true), overly broad permissions requests, WebView misconfiguration enabling JavaScript execution on untrusted content, disabled ASLR or stack canaries in native code, missing ProGuard/R8 obfuscation in release builds, and debug logging active in production. Platform-specific hardening features must be explicitly enabled: Android Network Security Config for certificate pinning, iOS Keychain access groups for credential isolation, and appropriate entitlements and capabilities restrictions. Regular security audits using mobile security testing frameworks (MobSF, MSTG) help identify misconfigurations.

name: Insecure Data Storage
description: Mobile devices store data in multiple locations that may be accessible to other applications, backup systems, or physical attackers. Insecure storage locations include plaintext SQLite databases, SharedPreferences/NSUserDefaults without encryption, application log files containing sensitive data, clipboard data accessible to other apps, external storage (SD card) readable by all apps, WebView caches and cookie stores, and keyboard caches that store entered text. On rooted/jailbroken devices, all application data becomes accessible. Even on non-compromised devices, application data may be extracted through device backups (iTunes, ADB), forensic tools, or exploiting backup-related vulnerabilities. Prevention requires using platform-provided secure storage (Android EncryptedSharedPreferences, iOS Keychain), encrypting SQLite databases (SQLCipher), clearing sensitive data from memory and caches, disabling keyboard caching for sensitive fields, and excluding sensitive data from backups.

name: Insufficient Cryptography
description: Mobile applications frequently implement cryptography incorrectly, undermining the security it is intended to provide. Common failures include using deprecated algorithms (DES, RC4, MD5 for security purposes), hardcoded encryption keys in application binaries (easily extracted through reverse engineering), insecure key storage (keys stored alongside encrypted data, keys in SharedPreferences), improper use of ECB mode for block ciphers (revealing patterns in encrypted data), missing or weak initialization vectors, insufficient key lengths, custom cryptographic implementations instead of well-tested libraries, and using Math.random() or java.util.Random for security-sensitive operations. The mobile context adds specific challenges: the need for offline encryption, key derivation from user credentials, and secure storage of cryptographic material. Prevention requires using platform cryptographic APIs (Android Keystore, iOS CryptoKit/Security framework), following NIST guidelines for algorithm selection, and using hardware-backed key storage when available.

name: Prompt Injection
description: Prompt injection is the most critical vulnerability in LLM applications, occurring when an attacker manipulates the model's behavior through crafted input that overrides the system prompt or intended instructions. Direct prompt injection involves user input that explicitly instructs the model to ignore previous instructions, reveal system prompts, or perform unauthorized actions. Indirect prompt injection embeds malicious instructions in external content (web pages, documents, emails) that the LLM processes as context, causing it to execute the attacker's instructions when processing that content. Techniques include instruction hijacking, context manipulation, payload splitting across multiple turns, encoding attacks (base64, ROT13), and multi-lingual injection. Successful exploitation can lead to sensitive data exfiltration, unauthorized actions through tool-use capabilities, system prompt disclosure, and bypassing content filtering. Defense requires input validation, output filtering, privilege separation for tool calls, and treating all LLM outputs as potentially compromised.

name: Sensitive Information Disclosure
description: LLM applications risk exposing sensitive information through multiple vectors: the model may reveal training data containing PII, proprietary code, or confidential business information through extraction techniques; system prompts containing business logic, API keys, or internal instructions can be leaked through prompt injection; retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems may return documents the user is not authorized to access; and conversation histories may be exposed through inadequate session management. Training data extraction attacks use carefully crafted prompts to elicit memorized data, including verbatim reproduction of private information seen during training. RAG-specific risks include document access control failures where the retrieval component ignores user permissions. Prevention requires implementing document-level access controls in RAG systems, filtering sensitive information from LLM outputs, avoiding including secrets in system prompts, encrypting conversation histories, and implementing data loss prevention guardrails.

name: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
description: LLM supply chains introduce unique risks beyond traditional software dependencies. Vulnerabilities exist in pre-trained model weights (models may contain backdoors or biases from training), fine-tuning datasets (poisoned data can alter model behavior), model hosting platforms (compromised model repositories like Hugging Face could serve backdoored models), training infrastructure (GPU cluster compromise during training), and third-party plugins or tools integrated with the LLM system. Model serialization formats (pickle files) can contain arbitrary code that executes during deserialization. A compromised foundation model affects all downstream applications built upon it. The opacity of model weights makes detecting supply chain compromises extremely difficult compared to traditional code review. Prevention requires verifying model provenance and integrity (model cards, checksums), scanning serialized models for malicious payloads, auditing fine-tuning datasets, using only trusted model sources, and implementing model behavior monitoring to detect drift indicating compromise.

name: Data and Model Poisoning
description: Data and model poisoning involves deliberately manipulating the training data, fine-tuning data, or model weights to alter the model's behavior in ways that benefit the attacker. Training data poisoning introduces manipulated examples that cause the model to produce biased, incorrect, or malicious outputs for specific triggers while maintaining normal performance otherwise. Fine-tuning poisoning through access to the fine-tuning process introduces targeted behavioral changes. Model poisoning directly modifies model weights or architecture against integrity. Backdoor attacks implant triggers (specific words, phrases, or patterns) that activate the poisoned behavior only when present. The impact includes biased outputs that manipulate decision-making, degraded model accuracy for specific demographics or topics, hidden behaviors activated by trigger phrases, and generation of harmful or misleading content. Prevention requires data provenance tracking, anomaly detection in training data, behavioral testing with known triggers, federated learning with Byzantine fault tolerance, and continuous monitoring of model outputs for unexpected behavior patterns.

name: Improper Output Handling
description: This vulnerability arises when LLM outputs are used in downstream systems without proper validation, sanitization, or encoding. Since LLMs can generate arbitrary text including code, markup, and structured data, their outputs may contain injection payloads when consumed by other components. Specific risks include LLM-generated content rendered in web pages without encoding (enabling XSS), LLM outputs used in SQL queries or system commands (enabling injection through the LLM as a proxy), LLM-generated code executed without review or sandboxing, LLM outputs used to construct API calls or file operations, and LLM responses containing markdown or HTML that bypass content filters when rendered. The LLM acts as an injection intermediary: indirect prompt injection inserts malicious instructions via external content, and improper output handling allows those injected payloads to affect downstream systems. Prevention requires treating all LLM output as untrusted, applying context-appropriate encoding before rendering, sandboxing any code execution, and implementing output validation against expected schemas.

name: Excessive Agency
description: Excessive agency occurs when LLM-based agents are granted capabilities, permissions, or autonomy beyond what is necessary for their intended function. As LLMs are integrated with tools (web browsing, code execution, file system access, API calls, database queries), each tool represents an attack surface that can be triggered through prompt injection or unintended model behavior. Risks include agents with write access when only read is needed, tools that execute actions without human confirmation, excessive permissions on integrated services (admin API keys instead of read-only), agents that can chain multiple high-impact actions autonomously, and insufficient rate limiting on agent actions. A prompt injection attack against an over-privileged agent can result in data deletion, unauthorized transactions, system compromise, or data exfiltration through the agent's tool capabilities. Prevention requires applying least privilege to all agent tools, implementing human-in-the-loop controls for sensitive actions, rate limiting agent actions, logging all tool invocations, and designing tool APIs that limit blast radius.

name: System Prompt Leakage
description: System prompt leakage occurs when the confidential instructions, context, or configuration provided in system prompts are disclosed to users or attackers. System prompts often contain business logic, content policies, behavioral guardrails, API integration details, and sometimes sensitive information like API endpoints or internal procedures. Attackers extract system prompts through direct requests ('repeat your instructions'), indirect techniques (asking the model to translate, summarize, or encode its instructions), conversation manipulation that causes the model to reference its instructions, and side-channel inference from model behavior patterns. While system prompt leakage alone may seem low impact, exposed prompts reveal the application's security controls (enabling targeted bypass), hidden capabilities, business logic that provides competitive advantage, and potential injection vectors for other attacks. Prevention requires assuming system prompts will be disclosed and not embedding secrets in them, implementing output filtering for prompt-like content, testing with adversarial prompt extraction techniques, and monitoring for system prompt content in model outputs.

name: Vector and Embedding Weaknesses
description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems and vector databases introduce unique security risks through their embedding and retrieval mechanisms. Vector poisoning attacks involve inserting carefully crafted documents that are semantically similar to target queries, ensuring they are retrieved and influence the model's responses with attacker-controlled content. Embedding inversion attacks attempt to recover original text from embedding vectors, potentially exposing sensitive document content. Access control failures in vector databases may allow users to retrieve documents they should not have access to, as vector similarity search may not respect document-level permissions. Additional risks include cross-tenant data leakage in shared vector databases, manipulation of retrieval relevance through adversarial document optimization, and metadata injection through document processing pipelines. Prevention requires implementing document-level access controls in vector retrieval, using separate vector stores per tenant, monitoring retrieval quality for anomalies, and sanitizing documents before embedding.

name: Misinformation
description: LLMs generate content that may be factually incorrect, misleading, or fabricated (hallucinated), and this misinformation can have serious consequences when used in decision-making, customer communication, medical or legal advice, or automated content generation. The model's confident and articulate presentation of false information makes it particularly dangerous as users may not verify LLM-generated claims. Specific risks include hallucinated references and citations that appear authoritative, confidently stated factual errors in domain-specific contexts (medical, legal, financial), generated content that reinforces biases present in training data, fabrication of events, statistics, or expert opinions, and automated generation of misleading content at scale. The risk is amplified when LLM outputs are published without human review, used in automated decision systems, or consumed by other AI systems in agent chains. Prevention requires implementing fact-checking mechanisms, grounding responses in retrieved documents (RAG), clearly communicating AI-generated content limitations, requiring human review for high-stakes outputs, and providing source attribution for claims.

name: Unbounded Consumption
description: LLM applications can be targeted with resource consumption attacks that exploit the high computational cost of model inference. Specific attacks include sending extremely long input prompts that consume excessive GPU memory and processing time, crafting inputs that trigger maximum-length responses draining compute resources, denial-of-wallet attacks that exploit pay-per-token pricing models to generate large bills, concurrent request floods that exhaust GPU capacity, recursive agent loops where the LLM repeatedly calls tools that call the LLM (infinite loops consuming resources), and prompt injection attacks that cause the model to generate extremely verbose responses. The high cost of GPU inference means even moderate attack volumes can generate significant financial impact. Prevention requires implementing input length limits, output token limits, per-user/per-API-key rate limiting, timeout mechanisms for inference requests, monitoring and alerting on abnormal consumption patterns, implementing circuit breakers for agent loops, and budgeting controls for pay-per-use LLM APIs.

name: Insecure Workload Configurations
description: The most common Kubernetes security issue, insecure workload configurations occur when pods and containers are deployed with security settings that provide excessive capabilities. Common misconfigurations include running containers as root (the default), mounting the Docker socket into containers (enabling container escape), using privileged containers or adding unnecessary Linux capabilities (SYS_ADMIN, NET_ADMIN), missing resource limits allowing resource exhaustion attacks, hostPath volumes exposing the node filesystem, hostNetwork/hostPID/hostIPC settings breaking namespace isolation, and missing securityContext configurations (readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation). Each misconfiguration weakens container isolation and enables escalation from container compromise to node compromise. Prevention requires implementing Pod Security Standards (restricted profile), using admission controllers (OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno) to enforce security policies, scanning workload configurations with tools like kubeaudit or Checkov, and establishing secure deployment templates.

name: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
description: Kubernetes supply chain vulnerabilities span the entire container lifecycle from base images through deployment. Risks include base images containing known vulnerabilities (outdated OS packages, unpatched libraries), malicious images from untrusted registries (typosquatting on Docker Hub), images without vulnerability scanning in CI/CD pipelines, missing image signing and verification (allowing image tampering), Dockerfile instructions that pull from unverified sources, large images with unnecessary attack surface (preferring distroless/minimal images), and build pipeline compromise injecting malicious code into container images. A single compromised container image propagates to every environment where it is deployed. Prevention requires using trusted base images from verified registries, implementing container image scanning (Trivy, Grype, Snyk Container), enforcing image signing with Cosign/Notary, using minimal base images (distroless, Alpine), implementing admission controllers that verify image signatures, and maintaining an SBOM for all deployed images.

name: Overly Permissive RBAC Configurations
description: Kubernetes RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) is the primary mechanism for controlling access to the Kubernetes API and resources, but overly permissive configurations are common due to complexity and the tendency to grant broad permissions for convenience. Dangerous patterns include cluster-admin role granted to service accounts or users who do not need full cluster access, wildcard permissions on resources and verbs ('*' on all resources), excessive use of ClusterRoles where namespace-scoped Roles would suffice, service accounts with unnecessary permissions (all pods inherit their namespace's default service account), and accumulation of permissions over time without review. An attacker who compromises a workload with overly permissive RBAC can escalate to cluster-admin, access secrets across namespaces, deploy malicious workloads, and pivot to other clusters. Prevention requires following least-privilege principles, auditing RBAC regularly with tools like rbac-lookup or rakkess, using namespace-scoped Roles instead of ClusterRoles, and implementing custom service accounts with minimal permissions for each workload.

name: Lack of Centralized Policy Enforcement
description: Without centralized policy enforcement, security policies must be implemented and verified at each deployment, leading to inconsistent security posture across the cluster. Admission controllers (OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno, Pod Security Admission) provide centralized policy enforcement by intercepting and validating resource creation requests before they are persisted. Without these controls, individual teams may deploy workloads with privileged containers, use images from untrusted registries, create resources without required labels or annotations for governance, deploy without network policies, or create overly permissive RBAC bindings. Centralized policies should enforce container security standards, image provenance requirements, resource limits, network policy presence, label requirements for governance, and compliance standards. Prevention requires deploying admission controller frameworks, defining policies as code for version control and review, testing policies in audit mode before enforcement, and implementing exception processes for legitimate policy overrides.

name: Inadequate Logging and Monitoring
description: Kubernetes environments generate logs from multiple sources (API server audit logs, container stdout/stderr, node-level system logs, network flow logs, admission controller decisions) and without comprehensive logging and monitoring, security incidents go undetected. Essential logging includes Kubernetes API audit logs capturing who performed what actions on which resources, container application logs with security-relevant events, pod lifecycle events, network flow logs from CNI plugins, admission controller decision logs, and secret access patterns. Common failures include disabled API audit logging, missing container log collection, insufficient log retention, no alerting on suspicious API patterns (secret enumeration, RBAC escalation attempts), and inability to correlate events across cluster components. Prevention requires enabling API audit logging with comprehensive policy, deploying centralized log collection (Fluentd/Fluent Bit, Loki), implementing runtime security monitoring (Falco, Tetragon), and establishing alerting rules for Kubernetes-specific attack patterns.

name: Broken Authentication Mechanisms
description: Kubernetes authentication weaknesses include insecure methods of proving identity to the Kubernetes API server and misconfigured authentication across the cluster ecosystem. Common issues include anonymous authentication enabled on the API server, use of static token files or basic authentication (deprecated but still functional), insecure distribution or storage of kubeconfig files containing cluster credentials, service account token auto-mounting to pods that do not need API access, missing or weak authentication on cluster components (etcd, kubelet API, dashboard), and long-lived service account tokens without rotation. Compromised authentication credentials provide direct access to the Kubernetes API, enabling full cluster compromise depending on associated RBAC permissions. Prevention requires disabling anonymous authentication, using short-lived certificate-based authentication or OIDC integration, implementing auto-mounting disabled by default for service accounts, securing all cluster component APIs, and rotating credentials regularly.

name: Missing Network Segmentation Controls
description: By default, all pods in a Kubernetes cluster can communicate with all other pods across namespaces, creating a flat network that allows lateral movement after initial compromise. Network Policies are the Kubernetes-native mechanism for implementing network segmentation, but they are often not implemented due to complexity and fear of breaking applications. Without network policies, a compromised pod can scan and communicate with every service in the cluster, access databases and caches directly, reach the Kubernetes API server, communicate with cloud metadata services, and pivot between namespaces. Effective network segmentation requires default-deny policies that block all traffic not explicitly allowed, namespace-level isolation for multi-tenant environments, restricting egress to prevent data exfiltration, and protecting cluster-internal services (etcd, kubelet) from workload access. Prevention requires implementing CNI plugins that support Network Policies (Calico, Cilium), establishing default-deny policies per namespace, and using tools like Network Policy Editor to visualize and validate policies.

name: Secrets Management Failures
description: Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded (not encrypted) by default and stored in etcd, creating multiple exposure points. Common failures include storing secrets in plain Kubernetes Secrets without encryption at rest, embedding secrets in container images or environment variables (visible in pod specs), insufficient RBAC restrictions allowing broad secret access, secrets committed to source code repositories, lack of secret rotation policies, using the same secrets across environments (dev/staging/prod), and accessing cloud provider secrets through node instance profiles rather than workload identity. Kubernetes etcd stores Secrets in cleartext by default, and anyone with etcd access can read all Secrets. Prevention requires enabling encryption at rest for etcd, using external secret management (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) with CSI Secret Store Driver, implementing workload identity for cloud provider access, restricting Secret access through RBAC, implementing automatic secret rotation, and auditing secret access patterns.

name: Misconfigured Cluster Components
description: Kubernetes clusters consist of multiple components (API server, etcd, kubelet, kube-proxy, controller-manager, scheduler) each with security-relevant configuration options that are often left at insecure defaults. Critical misconfigurations include etcd without mutual TLS authentication (enabling unauthorized data access), kubelet API without authentication (enabling remote code execution on nodes), API server with insecure-port enabled, kube-proxy configuration allowing localhost access to services, controller-manager or scheduler running with excessive permissions, insecure admission controller ordering, and cloud-specific misconfigurations (instance metadata accessible from pods, overly permissive security groups). Each component misconfiguration provides a different attack vector: etcd access yields all cluster secrets, kubelet access yields node-level code execution, and API server misconfig yields cluster-admin access. Prevention requires following CIS Kubernetes Benchmarks, using managed Kubernetes services with secure defaults where possible, implementing configuration drift detection, and regularly running kube-bench for compliance scanning.

name: Outdated and Vulnerable Kubernetes Components
description: Running outdated Kubernetes versions, node operating systems, and cluster add-ons exposes the cluster to known vulnerabilities with published exploits. Kubernetes releases security patches frequently, and older versions reach end-of-life (typically n-3 minor versions). Notable Kubernetes CVEs include container escape vulnerabilities (CVE-2019-5736 runc escape, CVE-2022-0185 filesystem context escape), privilege escalation via API abuse (CVE-2018-1002105 API server proxy escalation), and node compromise through kubelet vulnerabilities. Beyond the core platform, add-ons (Ingress controllers, CNI plugins, service meshes, monitoring tools) and the container runtime (containerd, CRI-O) also require regular patching. Prevention requires establishing a Kubernetes upgrade cadence aligned with the support lifecycle, monitoring security advisories for all cluster components, implementing automated vulnerability scanning for node OS and container runtime, and testing upgrades in staging environments before production rollout.

name: Insufficient Flow Control Mechanisms
description: This risk addresses the lack of proper enforcement mechanisms within CI/CD pipelines that ensure code changes undergo required review, approval, and testing processes before reaching production. Without sufficient flow control, attackers who compromise any single component in the pipeline can push malicious code directly to production, bypassing code review, security scanning, and testing stages. Common weaknesses include missing branch protection rules allowing direct pushes to main branches, self-approved pull requests, missing or bypassable required status checks, pipeline stages that can be manually skipped by developers, and deployment pipelines that do not verify the complete chain of approvals. A single compromised developer account can push backdoored code to production if flow controls are insufficient. Prevention requires enforcing branch protection with required approvals from multiple reviewers, mandatory status checks that cannot be bypassed, environment-specific deployment gates, and separation of duties between code authoring and deployment approval.

name: Inadequate Identity and Access Management
description: CI/CD systems involve multiple identities (human users, service accounts, machine identities, API tokens) across multiple platforms (source control, build systems, artifact registries, deployment targets) creating a complex identity management challenge. Common failures include shared service accounts used across multiple pipelines, overly permissive access to pipeline configuration, human accounts with direct deployment access bypassing pipelines, inactive or orphaned accounts retaining access, missing MFA on CI/CD platform accounts, and excessive permissions on CI/CD service identities (admin access to cloud platforms). The CI/CD system often has the most privileged access in the organization as it needs to deploy to production environments, making it a high-value target. Prevention requires implementing unique service accounts per pipeline with least-privilege permissions, enforcing MFA on all human accounts, regular access reviews, just-in-time access for elevated permissions, and federated identity management across CI/CD platforms.

name: Dependency Chain Abuse
description: CI/CD pipelines automatically resolve and install dependencies during build processes, creating opportunities for supply chain attacks through the dependency resolution mechanism. Attack vectors include dependency confusion (publishing public packages with the same name as private packages), typosquatting (publishing packages with names similar to popular packages), compromised maintainer accounts pushing malicious updates to legitimate packages, dependency hijacking (taking over abandoned package names), and malicious post-install scripts that execute during package installation. The build environment often has access to secrets, source code, and deployment credentials, making it a high-value target for dependency attacks. Notable incidents include the event-stream npm compromise, ua-parser-js hijacking, and colors.js/faker.js sabotage. Prevention requires using private registries with namespace scoping, implementing dependency pinning with lock files, scanning dependencies for known vulnerabilities and malicious indicators, configuring package managers to fail on unexpected dependency sources, and using dependency firewalls that proxy and analyze packages.

name: Poisoned Pipeline Execution (PPE)
description: Poisoned Pipeline Execution occurs when attackers manipulate the CI/CD pipeline configuration or build process to execute malicious code within the pipeline environment. There are three main variants: Direct PPE where attackers modify the pipeline configuration file (Jenkinsfile, .github/workflows, .gitlab-ci.yml) in a branch or pull request; Indirect PPE where attackers modify files referenced by the pipeline (build scripts, test configurations, Makefiles) without changing the pipeline definition; and Public PPE where pipelines triggered by public repositories execute untrusted code with access to internal secrets. PPE is particularly dangerous because pipeline execution environments typically have access to deployment credentials, secrets management systems, artifact registries, and production infrastructure. Prevention requires separating pipeline definition from code repositories (defining pipelines in platform configuration rather than repository files), using ephemeral pipeline runners, restricting secrets exposure to specific pipeline stages, and implementing approval gates for pipeline configuration changes.

name: Insufficient PBAC (Pipeline-Based Access Controls)
description: Pipeline-Based Access Controls define what resources, environments, and actions each pipeline is authorized to access and perform. Without PBAC, any pipeline can potentially access any secret, deploy to any environment, or interact with any service. Common failures include shared credentials available to all pipelines regardless of their purpose, pipelines with unrestricted network access to production infrastructure, build pipelines that can also perform deployments (lack of stage separation), pipelines across different projects sharing the same runner infrastructure without isolation, and missing environment-specific deployment restrictions. An attacker who compromises a low-criticality pipeline can pivot to access production secrets and deployment targets if PBAC is insufficient. Prevention requires implementing pipeline-level access controls for secrets and credentials, using dedicated runner pools for different security zones, restricting deployment pipelines to specific environments, and implementing network segmentation between pipeline stages.

name: Insufficient Credential Hygiene
description: CI/CD systems handle large numbers of credentials (API keys, deployment tokens, cloud provider credentials, registry passwords, SSH keys, signing certificates) and poor credential management creates significant exposure. Common issues include hardcoded credentials in pipeline configuration files, secrets stored in plaintext in CI/CD platform variables without masking, credentials with overly broad permissions, long-lived credentials without rotation, shared credentials used across multiple pipelines and environments, secrets printed in build logs, and credentials persisted in container images or build artifacts. The CI/CD environment is a high-value target because it aggregates credentials for accessing multiple systems across the software delivery lifecycle. Prevention requires using external secrets management systems, implementing short-lived dynamic credentials, enabling credential rotation, masking secrets in build logs, scanning code and artifacts for accidentally committed credentials, and implementing separate credentials per pipeline and environment.

name: Insecure System Configuration
description: CI/CD platforms (Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps) have their own configuration surfaces that must be hardened. Common misconfigurations include unprotected CI/CD administrative interfaces, self-hosted runners with excessive system access, missing or outdated platform patches, insecure default configurations (Jenkins script console accessible, anonymous read access), disabled security features (unverified webhooks, disabled audit logging), misconfigured webhook secrets allowing unauthorized pipeline triggers, and insecure plugin or extension configurations. Self-hosted CI/CD runners are particularly vulnerable as they may accumulate state between builds (cached credentials, build artifacts with secrets), have direct network access to internal infrastructure, and run with elevated operating system privileges. Prevention requires following vendor hardening guides, using ephemeral and isolated runners (containers or VMs destroyed after each build), enabling audit logging, restricting administrative access, keeping platforms updated, and regularly reviewing platform configuration.

name: Ungoverned Usage of Third-Party Services
description: Modern CI/CD pipelines integrate with numerous third-party services (GitHub Actions marketplace, Jenkins shared libraries, cloud marketplace offerings, SaaS integrations) that run code within the pipeline with access to its credentials and resources. Common risks include using unvetted GitHub Actions from the marketplace that could be compromised, referencing third-party pipeline templates without version pinning, granting third-party services excessive OAuth permissions, integrating SaaS tools with access to source code or build artifacts, and using unverified container images as build environments. Third-party service compromise provides attackers direct code execution within the pipeline with access to all pipeline secrets and permissions. Prevention requires maintaining an approved list of third-party CI/CD integrations, pinning specific versions of external actions and templates (using commit SHA rather than tags), reviewing third-party service permissions regularly, implementing approval workflows for new integrations, and monitoring third-party service behavior for anomalies.

name: Improper Artifact Integrity Validation
description: Build artifacts (container images, packages, binaries) move through multiple stages from build to deployment, and without integrity validation at each stage, artifacts can be modified or replaced with malicious versions. Risks include deploying container images without signature verification, accepting artifacts from build systems without provenance attestation, using artifact registries that do not enforce integrity checks, missing integrity verification between build and deployment stages, and pulling dependencies from registries without hash verification. An attacker who gains access to the artifact registry or can perform man-in-the-middle attacks between build and deployment can replace legitimate artifacts with backdoored versions. Prevention requires implementing artifact signing (Cosign, Notary) with verification at deployment, generating and verifying build provenance attestations (SLSA framework), using content-addressable storage for artifacts, implementing admission controllers that verify signatures before deployment, and maintaining chain-of-custody records for all artifacts.

name: Insufficient Logging and Visibility
description: Without comprehensive logging and monitoring of CI/CD activities, organizations cannot detect unauthorized pipeline modifications, suspicious build behaviors, credential misuse, or supply chain attacks in progress. Essential CI/CD logs include pipeline configuration changes, execution history with inputs and outputs, secret access and credential usage patterns, artifact registry operations (push, pull, delete), access control changes and authentication events, integration with external services, and runner provisioning and lifecycle events. Common failures include missing audit logs for pipeline modifications, build logs that are overwritten or expire before investigation, no alerting on unusual pipeline behavior (builds at unusual times, unexpected deployment targets), and inability to correlate CI/CD events with application security incidents. Prevention requires enabling comprehensive audit logging across all CI/CD platforms, centralizing CI/CD logs with application and infrastructure logs, establishing baselines for normal pipeline behavior, and implementing alerts for anomalous activities.

name: Insecure Code Review Process
description: The code review process is a critical security control in the software delivery pipeline, and weaknesses in this process allow malicious or vulnerable code to reach production. Common failures include pull requests approved without meaningful review (rubber-stamping), insufficient reviewer expertise in security-relevant code changes, missing automated security scanning (SAST, SCA) as part of the review process, review requirements that can be bypassed by repository administrators, no separation between code authors and reviewers, large pull requests that discourage thorough review, and missing review requirements for infrastructure-as-code and pipeline configuration changes. The code review process is often the primary gate preventing supply chain attacks through insider threats or compromised developer accounts. Prevention requires enforcing minimum reviewer counts with appropriate expertise requirements, integrating automated security scanning as required status checks, implementing code owners for security-sensitive files, limiting self-merge capabilities, and training reviewers on common security patterns and anti-patterns.

name: Insecure Cloud Identity and Access Management
description: Cloud IAM is the foundation of cloud security, controlling who can access what resources with what permissions. Misconfigurations are the leading cause of cloud breaches. Common issues include overly permissive IAM policies using wildcard permissions (Action: *, Resource: *), long-lived access keys without rotation, missing or disabled MFA for privileged accounts, improperly configured cross-account access, excessive permissions on service roles, IAM policies attached directly to users rather than groups or roles, and insufficient use of temporary credentials (AssumeRole). Cloud IAM complexity is amplified by the interaction between identity policies, resource policies, permission boundaries, session policies, and service control policies, where a permissive configuration in any layer can create unauthorized access. Prevention requires implementing least privilege using tools like IAM Access Analyzer, enforcing MFA for all human users, eliminating long-lived access keys in favor of temporary credentials, conducting regular permission audits, and using infrastructure-as-code for IAM policy management.

name: Insecure Cloud Storage
description: Cloud storage services (S3, Azure Blob, GCS) are frequently misconfigured, exposing sensitive data to unauthorized access. This has led to some of the largest data breaches in history, exposing billions of records across healthcare, government, financial, and consumer sectors. Common misconfigurations include publicly accessible storage buckets or containers, overly broad access policies granting world-readable or world-writable access, missing encryption for sensitive data at rest, bucket policies that override account-level protections, disabled access logging making breach detection impossible, missing versioning allowing permanent data loss, and cross-account access without proper controls. The shared responsibility model means cloud providers secure the storage infrastructure but customers are responsible for access configuration. Prevention requires enforcing block public access at the account level, implementing bucket policies with explicit deny rules, enabling access logging and monitoring, requiring encryption at rest, using pre-signed URLs for temporary access instead of broad permissions, and deploying automated continuous monitoring for misconfigured storage.

name: Insecure Cloud Networking
description: Cloud networking security involves configuring virtual networks, security groups, network ACLs, and connectivity to prevent unauthorized network access to cloud resources. Common misconfigurations include security groups with overly broad inbound rules (0.0.0.0/0 on SSH, RDP, or database ports), missing network segmentation between application tiers, public subnets used for resources that should be private, unused elastic IPs or public IP addresses attached to internal services, misconfigured VPC peering or transit gateways creating unintended connectivity, missing flow log collection for network traffic analysis, and overly permissive outbound rules allowing data exfiltration. Cloud networking differs from traditional networking because changes can be made programmatically and instantly, increasing the speed at which misconfigurations can be introduced. Prevention requires implementing default-deny security groups, using private subnets with NAT gateways for internet access, enabling VPC flow logs, implementing network segmentation between tiers, and using infrastructure-as-code with security guardrails for networking changes.

name: Insufficient Cloud Logging and Monitoring
description: Cloud environments generate extensive telemetry data across multiple services, and without comprehensive logging and monitoring, security incidents go undetected. Essential cloud logging includes cloud trail/audit logs capturing all API calls, VPC flow logs for network traffic analysis, storage access logs, database audit logs, WAF logs, DNS query logs, and application-level logs. Common failures include disabled or partially configured audit logging (CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, GCP Audit Logs), missing log centralization and correlation across multiple accounts and regions, insufficient log retention for forensic analysis, no real-time alerting on high-risk events (root account usage, security group changes, IAM policy modifications), and logs siloed in individual service consoles without cross-service correlation. Prevention requires enabling comprehensive audit logging across all cloud services, aggregating logs in a centralized SIEM, establishing alerting rules for high-risk cloud events, implementing automated response to critical events, and conducting regular log review as part of security operations.

name: Cloud Metadata Service Abuse
description: Cloud instance metadata services (IMDS) provide instances with information about themselves including IAM credentials, network configuration, user data scripts, and instance identity documents. These services are accessible at well-known endpoints (169.254.169.254 for AWS/Azure, metadata.google.internal for GCP) from any process running on the instance. SSRF vulnerabilities in web applications running on cloud instances can be exploited to access metadata endpoints and steal IAM credentials, which often have broad permissions. The Capital One breach (2019) famously exploited SSRF to access AWS metadata credentials. AWS IMDSv2 mitigates basic SSRF attacks by requiring session tokens obtained through PUT requests, but not all workloads have migrated. Additional risks include user data scripts containing secrets accessible through metadata, instance profiles with excessive permissions, and container escape scenarios where metadata service access provides cloud-level privileges. Prevention requires enforcing IMDSv2 (AWS), implementing metadata concealment (GKE), using pod identity (AKS), restricting metadata access through network policies, and minimizing instance role permissions.

name: Insecure Cloud Serverless Configuration
description: Serverless functions (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions) introduce unique security challenges due to their ephemeral nature, event-driven invocation, and distributed architecture. Common vulnerabilities include overly permissive execution roles granting serverless functions access to resources far beyond their requirements, sensitive data (API keys, database credentials) stored in plaintext environment variables visible in deployment configuration, insecure event source configuration allowing unauthorized function invocation, inadequate function timeout and memory settings enabling denial-of-wallet attacks, missing input validation on event payloads from various triggers (API Gateway, S3, SQS, SNS), cold start credential caching that persists across unrelated invocations, and deployment package vulnerabilities from outdated dependencies. Serverless architectures complicate traditional security monitoring because the ephemeral execution environment prevents agent-based monitoring. Prevention requires applying least-privilege IAM to each function, using secrets manager for credentials, validating all event inputs, implementing function-level monitoring and tracing, setting appropriate timeouts and concurrency limits, and scanning deployment packages for vulnerabilities.

name: Cloud Resource Enumeration and Discovery
description: Cloud environments can be systematically enumerated by attackers to discover exposed resources, services, and attack surfaces. Enumeration techniques include scanning for publicly accessible cloud storage (S3 bucket names follow predictable naming conventions), discovering exposed cloud services through DNS enumeration, identifying cloud provider and region through HTTP headers and response characteristics, enumerating API endpoints through public documentation or error messages, discovering cloud function URLs through predictable naming, and identifying cloud database endpoints exposed to the internet. Cloud-specific reconnaissance tools (cloud_enum, ScoutSuite, Prowler) automate the discovery of misconfigurations across cloud services. The programmatic nature of cloud resource creation means misconfigured resources can be created at scale, multiplying the attack surface. Prevention requires avoiding predictable resource naming, minimizing publicly discoverable endpoints, implementing cloud security posture management (CSPM) tools for continuous monitoring, conducting regular external attack surface assessments, and removing unnecessary public exposure of cloud resources.

name: Weak or Guessable Passwords
description: IoT devices frequently ship with default credentials (admin/admin, root/root, or device-model-specific defaults) that users are not required to change, and many devices have hardcoded credentials that cannot be changed at all. The Mirai botnet demonstrated the devastating scale of this issue, compromising hundreds of thousands of IoT devices using a list of just 62 default username/password combinations to create a massive DDoS botnet. Beyond default credentials, IoT password weaknesses include lack of account lockout mechanisms enabling brute-force attacks, password recovery mechanisms that reset to known defaults, shared credentials across all devices of the same model, backdoor accounts for manufacturer support, and firmware-embedded service credentials discoverable through reverse engineering. Prevention requires unique per-device credentials generated during manufacturing, mandatory password change on first use, enforcement of minimum password complexity, rate limiting and account lockout for authentication, and eliminating hardcoded or backdoor credentials entirely.

name: Insecure Network Services
description: IoT devices often run unnecessary or insecure network services that expand the attack surface. Common issues include running Telnet instead of SSH for remote management, exposing UPnP services that enable device discovery and control from the network, running unpatched web servers for device administration, open debug ports (JTAG, serial console) accessible via network, services listening on all interfaces when only local access is needed, unencrypted protocols for device communication (MQTT without TLS, CoAP without DTLS), and firmware update services without authentication. Resource-constrained devices may use lightweight protocol implementations with security vulnerabilities that would be caught in more mature server software. Services running on IoT devices are frequently not updated after deployment, accumulating vulnerabilities over the device lifetime. Prevention requires disabling all unnecessary services, using encrypted protocols for all communications, implementing proper authentication on all services, restricting network exposure to minimum necessary interfaces, and conducting regular network scans of deployed device fleets.

name: Insecure Ecosystem Interfaces
description: IoT ecosystems include multiple interfaces beyond the device itself: cloud APIs, mobile applications, web interfaces, local network APIs, and inter-device communication protocols. Each interface represents an attack surface with potentially different security postures. Common vulnerabilities include web administration interfaces with XSS, CSRF, and injection flaws; mobile applications that hardcode API keys or lack certificate pinning; cloud APIs with broken authentication or authorization; local APIs (REST, MQTT, CoAP) without authentication; and third-party integrations (Alexa, Google Home, IFTTT) with excessive permissions. An attacker who compromises any ecosystem interface may gain control of the device, access collected data, or pivot to other devices and networks. Prevention requires security testing of all ecosystem interfaces, implementing consistent authentication and authorization across interfaces, encrypting all communication channels, following API security best practices for cloud interfaces, and applying mobile security controls to companion applications.

name: Lack of Secure Update Mechanism
description: IoT devices require firmware and software updates throughout their operational lifetime to address security vulnerabilities, but many devices lack secure, reliable update mechanisms. Common failures include no update capability at all (requiring physical replacement for security fixes), updates transmitted over unencrypted channels (enabling MITM injection of malicious firmware), missing cryptographic verification of update integrity and authenticity (enabling firmware replacement attacks), update mechanisms that brick devices on failure (deterring users from applying updates), no automatic update capability requiring manual intervention, and update servers that are no longer maintained. Without secure updates, every vulnerability discovered after deployment remains permanently exploitable across all affected devices.
