Prevention requires implementing signed firmware updates with cryptographic verification, supporting automatic background updates, designing rollback capability for failed updates, using TLS for update delivery, implementing A/B partition schemes for safe updates, and committing to long-term update support.

name: Use of Insecure or Outdated Components
description: IoT devices frequently use outdated software components with known vulnerabilities due to long product development cycles, limited computational resources that prevent running modern software, and lack of regular update processes. Common issues include outdated Linux kernels with known privilege escalation vulnerabilities, legacy versions of OpenSSL, BusyBox, or other critical libraries, deprecated protocol implementations (SSLv3, TLS 1.0), custom firmware based on abandoned open-source projects, and binary components without source code access preventing patching. The long operational lifetime of IoT devices (10-20+ years for industrial and infrastructure devices) exacerbates this issue as software that was current at deployment becomes increasingly vulnerable. Supply chain complexity adds risk as components may include nested dependencies from multiple vendors. Prevention requires maintaining a software bill of materials for all device firmware, implementing automated vulnerability scanning in the firmware build process, establishing component update policies throughout the device lifecycle, and selecting components with long-term support commitments.

name: Insufficient Privacy Protection
description: IoT devices collect intimate personal data through environmental sensors, cameras, microphones, health monitors, and behavioral tracking that creates comprehensive profiles of users' daily lives. Privacy failures include collecting more data than necessary for device functionality, transmitting personal data to cloud services without user consent or awareness, sharing data with third parties (advertisers, analytics providers) without disclosure, storing personal data indefinitely without retention policies, lacking data export or deletion capabilities (right to erasure), missing or inadequate privacy policies that do not accurately describe data practices, and cross-device data correlation that creates detailed behavioral profiles. Smart home devices, wearables, and connected vehicles are particularly sensitive as they operate in private spaces and track movement patterns. Prevention requires implementing privacy by design (data minimization, purpose limitation), providing transparent privacy controls, supporting data export and deletion, complying with applicable privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA), and conducting privacy impact assessments for new data collection.

name: Insecure Data Transfer and Storage
description: IoT devices handle sensitive data including personal information, environmental data, access credentials, and operational telemetry that must be protected both in transit and at rest. Common failures include transmitting data over unencrypted protocols (HTTP, MQTT without TLS, plaintext serial), storing credentials and sensitive data in plaintext on the device filesystem, using weak or broken encryption algorithms (not supported by modern standards), hardcoded encryption keys identical across all devices of the same model, insufficient protection of data in cloud backends (unencrypted databases, publicly accessible storage), and missing integrity verification allowing data tampering. Resource-constrained devices may lack the processing power for strong encryption, but modern IoT-appropriate cryptographic implementations (TLS 1.3, DTLS, hardware crypto acceleration) address this concern. Prevention requires encrypting all data in transit using TLS/DTLS, encrypting sensitive data at rest, using hardware-backed secure storage where available, implementing unique per-device encryption keys, and applying appropriate data protection to cloud backends.

name: Lack of Device Management
description: Large IoT deployments require comprehensive device management capabilities for security maintenance throughout the device lifecycle. Without proper device management, organizations cannot track deployed devices, monitor their security status, deploy updates, or respond to incidents. Common failures include no central inventory of deployed devices (shadow IoT), inability to remotely monitor device health and security status, missing capability for remote configuration changes, no mechanism for device decommissioning and data wiping, inability to enforce security policies across the device fleet, and missing device authentication preventing rogue device detection. Unmanaged devices become permanent vulnerabilities as they cannot be patched, monitored, or decommissioned. Prevention requires implementing a device management platform with asset inventory, supporting remote monitoring and management protocols, implementing device attestation for fleet integrity, establishing device lifecycle policies (provisioning, monitoring, patching, decommissioning), and maintaining accurate records of all deployed devices and their software versions.

name: Insecure Default Settings
description: IoT devices are often deployed with their default configuration, and if defaults are insecure, the devices remain vulnerable for their entire operational life. Common insecure defaults include network services enabled that are not needed by most users, permissive firewall rules allowing broad network access, debug interfaces (UART, JTAG, SSH with default keys) left active, verbose logging enabled (potentially exposing sensitive data), weak or no authentication on management interfaces, unnecessary user accounts with known credentials, and maximum privilege levels enabled by default. The secure-by-default principle requires that devices ship in their most secure configuration, requiring users to explicitly reduce security for specific use cases rather than requiring users to harden insecure defaults. Prevention requires shipping devices with minimum necessary services enabled, requiring authentication on all interfaces by default, disabling debug and development features in production firmware, implementing secure default network configurations, and providing easy-to-follow hardening guides for any necessary configuration changes.

name: Lack of Physical Hardening
description: IoT devices are frequently deployed in physically accessible or unattended locations where attackers can interact with the hardware directly. Physical attack vectors include accessing debug interfaces (JTAG, UART, SWD) to extract firmware and credentials, dumping flash memory to obtain encryption keys and proprietary algorithms, glitching attacks (voltage, clock, electromagnetic) to bypass secure boot or extract protected data, side-channel attacks (power analysis, electromagnetic emissions) to extract cryptographic keys, and physical modification to install hardware implants or bypass tamper protection. Industrial IoT devices, smart meters, ATMs, and outdoor infrastructure are particularly exposed. Even consumer devices like smart locks and security cameras may be physically accessible to attackers. Prevention requires disabling or removing debug interfaces in production devices, implementing secure boot chains that detect firmware tampering, using tamper-evident and tamper-resistant enclosures, storing sensitive data in hardware security modules (TPM, secure enclaves), and implementing device attestation that detects hardware modification.

name: Thick Client Broken Access Control
description: Desktop thick client applications often implement access control logic on the client side, trusting the application to enforce restrictions rather than the server. Attackers can bypass client-side access controls by modifying application binaries, intercepting and modifying network requests to backend APIs, manipulating local data stores that track user permissions, using debuggers to alter runtime behavior, or decompiling and recompiling the application with restrictions removed. Common patterns include menu items or features hidden but accessible through direct API calls, client-side role checks that are not enforced server-side, locally cached permission lists that can be edited, and trust placed in client-side validation for security decisions. Thick client architectures where business logic runs locally are inherently more vulnerable to access control bypass than thin client architectures. Prevention requires implementing all authorization checks on the server backend, treating thick client requests as untrusted, implementing API-level access controls independent of client UI restrictions, and using code obfuscation and integrity checking to raise the bypass difficulty.

name: Thick Client Weak Cryptography
description: Desktop applications frequently implement cryptographic operations locally for data protection, license validation, and secure communication, but commonly use weak or incorrect cryptographic implementations. Issues include using deprecated algorithms (DES, RC4, MD5), hardcoded encryption keys embedded in binaries that are extractable through reverse engineering, custom encryption schemes that have not been cryptographically analyzed, improper use of cryptographic primitives (ECB mode, missing initialization vectors, deterministic encryption), storing encryption keys alongside encrypted data, using the same key for encryption and authentication, and relying on obfuscation rather than proper encryption. Desktop applications are particularly vulnerable because the entire application binary, including embedded keys, is available to the attacker for analysis. Prevention requires using platform-provided cryptographic APIs (Windows DPAPI, macOS Keychain, libsodium), storing keys in hardware-backed secure storage (TPM, Secure Enclave), using well-established algorithms with proper parameters, and keeping encryption keys separate from encrypted data using key management services where possible.

name: Thick Client Injection
description: Desktop applications are susceptible to various injection attacks through their user interfaces, file handling, inter-process communication, and backend API interactions. Injection vectors include SQL injection through local SQLite databases, command injection through application features that spawn system processes, DLL injection by placing malicious libraries in the application search path, XML injection through configuration or data files, LDAP injection through Active Directory integration, and injection through IPC mechanisms (named pipes, COM objects, RPC). Desktop applications often have deeper system integration than web applications, meaning successful injection can provide broader access to the operating system, filesystem, and network resources. Additionally, desktop applications may process various file formats (CSV, XML, JSON, Office documents) that can contain injection payloads. Prevention requires using parameterized queries for all database interactions, validating and sanitizing all input regardless of source, using safe API alternatives instead of command-line execution, implementing DLL search path hardening, and applying input validation to file parsing and IPC inputs.

name: Thick Client Improper Session Management
description: Desktop applications manage user sessions differently from web applications, using local credential caching, persistent authentication tokens, and often maintaining long-lived sessions that survive application restarts. Common vulnerabilities include storing session tokens or credentials in plaintext in configuration files, registry entries, or local databases; not implementing session timeout or re-authentication for sensitive operations; session tokens that are not invalidated on the server when the user logs out; sharing sessions across multiple instances of the application; reusing the same session token across application restarts without freshness verification; and storing refresh tokens insecurely allowing persistent unauthorized access. In corporate environments, desktop applications often integrate with SSO systems (Kerberos, SAML, OAuth) and improper implementation can lead to token replay attacks, session fixation, or credential persistence beyond the intended session lifetime. Prevention requires using secure credential storage (Windows Credential Manager, macOS Keychain), implementing session timeouts and re-authentication, proper server-side session invalidation, and avoiding persistent credential storage when possible.

name: Thick Client Insecure Data Storage
description: Desktop applications store data in numerous local locations including application directories, user profile folders, temporary directories, registry entries, local databases (SQLite, LevelDB), configuration files, log files, cache directories, and crash reports. Each storage location may contain sensitive data (credentials, personal information, business data, encryption keys) that is vulnerable to access by other local users, malware, or physical attackers. Common failures include storing sensitive data in world-readable locations, using reversible encryption with embedded keys for local data protection, leaving sensitive data in temporary files or swap space, including personal data in crash reports sent to telemetry services, not clearing sensitive data from memory when no longer needed (leaving it vulnerable to memory dump attacks), and caching sensitive data in browser components (Electron, WebView2, CEF). Prevention requires using platform-provided secure storage (DPAPI, Keychain), encrypting sensitive local databases, implementing proper data lifecycle management (secure deletion, memory clearing), restricting file permissions on application data, and auditing all local storage locations for sensitive data exposure.

name: Adversarial Input Perturbation
description: Adversarial perturbation attacks craft input data with carefully calculated modifications that cause machine learning models to produce incorrect outputs while appearing normal to humans. In image classification, imperceptible pixel-level changes can cause a model to misclassify a stop sign as a speed limit sign with high confidence. In natural language processing, strategic word substitutions or character-level changes can fool sentiment analysis, spam filters, and content moderation systems. In malware detection, adversarial modifications to malicious binaries can evade ML-based security tools while preserving malicious functionality. Attack methods include gradient-based attacks (FGSM, PGD, C&W), black-box attacks using transferability between models, and physical-world attacks (adversarial patches, 3D-printed objects). The reliability of ML systems in safety-critical applications (autonomous vehicles, medical diagnosis, security screening) is fundamentally challenged by adversarial vulnerability. Prevention requires adversarial training (augmenting training data with adversarial examples), input preprocessing and anomaly detection, ensemble methods that are harder to simultaneously fool, certified defense techniques with provable robustness bounds, and human-in-the-loop verification for high-stakes decisions.

name: Data Poisoning
description: Data poisoning attacks compromise the integrity of machine learning models by manipulating training data to introduce targeted behaviors, backdoors, or degraded performance. Unlike adversarial evasion which operates at inference time, poisoning attacks affect the model during training, creating persistent vulnerabilities embedded in the model weights. Attack variants include label flipping (changing labels on training examples to introduce systematic errors), backdoor insertion (associating a trigger pattern with a target output so the model behaves normally except when the trigger is present), and clean-label attacks (adding specially crafted examples with correct labels that still shift the decision boundary). In federated learning, malicious participants can submit poisoned model updates. Data poisoning is particularly dangerous because compromised models may pass standard accuracy benchmarks while containing hidden vulnerabilities. Prevention requires data provenance tracking, statistical analysis of training data for anomalies, robust training algorithms (certified defenses, data sanitization), monitoring model behavior for unexpected patterns, and maintaining clean validation datasets separate from training data.

name: Model Inversion
description: Model inversion attacks exploit a machine learning model to reconstruct or infer information about data used for training, violating the privacy of individuals whose data was included in the training set. By systematically querying the model and analyzing its outputs, attackers can reconstruct approximations of training data including faces, medical records, or other sensitive information. Confidence-based inversion uses the model's prediction probabilities to iteratively refine a reconstruction toward the training distribution. Gradient-based inversion leverages model gradients (available in white-box settings or federated learning) to recover original training samples. Even with black-box access, repeated queries can extract meaningful information about training data distributions. Model inversion is particularly concerning for models trained on sensitive data (medical imaging, biometric recognition, financial records) as it can violate patient privacy, enable identity theft, or expose confidential business data. Prevention requires implementing differential privacy during training, limiting prediction confidence output, restricting query rates, applying output perturbation, and minimizing training on unnecessarily sensitive data.

name: Membership Inference
description: Membership inference attacks determine whether a specific data record was included in the model's training dataset. While seemingly limited in impact, this can reveal sensitive information: determining that a patient's records were used to train a disease prediction model implies they have the disease; confirming that an individual's data was in a credit model's training set may reveal financial history. Attackers exploit the observable difference in model behavior between training data (which the model has memorized to some degree) and unseen data, using shadow models trained on similar data to learn these distinguishing patterns. Overfitted models are particularly vulnerable as they exhibit larger behavioral differences between training and non-training data. Advanced variants can not only determine membership but also infer specific attributes of training samples. Prevention requires training with differential privacy guarantees, implementing regularization to prevent overfitting, limiting prediction output precision (rounding confidence scores), monitoring for systematic querying patterns, and applying model distillation to reduce memorization of training data.

name: Model Theft
description: Model theft (also called model extraction or model stealing) involves recreating a proprietary machine learning model's functionality through systematic querying of its API. Attackers submit carefully chosen inputs, observe the model's predictions, and train a surrogate model that approximates the original model's behavior. Stolen models can be used to generate adversarial examples that transfer to the original model, replicate a competitor's AI capabilities without their R&D investment, circumvent usage-based pricing by running the stolen model locally, and analyze the model's decision boundary to find exploitable weaknesses. Efficient extraction techniques require only a fraction of the queries needed to train the original model from scratch. The stolen model need not be architecturally identical to the original; a simpler model trained on the original's outputs can achieve comparable performance for the target task. Prevention requires implementing rate limiting and query budgeting, monitoring for distribution shift in query patterns (extraction attempts often use out-of-distribution inputs), watermarking model outputs (adding detectable patterns to predictions that survive extraction), requiring authentication and tracking API usage per user, and implementing prediction perturbation that degrades extraction quality while maintaining utility for legitimate users.
